<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: open-research-agenda-and-bibliography-plan
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/open-research-agenda-and-bibliography-plan.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/open-research-agenda-and-bibliography-plan.qmd@04b32c959
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Open Research Agenda and Bibliography Plan

The book should not end by pretending the work is finished.

The living-book method gives the project a way to change honestly. The open
research agenda gives that method somewhere to point. It turns incompleteness
into routed work rather than atmosphere: a source to fetch, a citation to
check, a claim to keep weak, a proof target to sharpen, a benchmark to rerun,
an artifact to import, or a chapter boundary to reconsider.

If the ASI Stack is a governed research program, its final chapter has to name
the unfinished work as carefully as the earlier chapters name mechanisms. A
book like this can fail at the very end by turning open questions into a vague
gesture: more papers to read, more tests to run, more proofs to write, more
systems to build. That is not enough. Future work has to be routed.

The open research agenda is that routing layer. It says which sources have
been loaded, which sources remain private or connector-bound, which external
literature has only been noted, which claims are still blocked, which proof or
test lane could move them, and when a new paper should update an existing
chapter instead of creating another one.

The bibliography is not a trophy case. It is an intake system.

That distinction keeps the book corrigible after publication. A future reader,
reviewer, or AI assistant should be able to ask a simple question: what would
have to happen before this claim gets stronger? The answer should not depend
on memory, enthusiasm, or scattered notes. It should be visible in the source
inventory, source notes, claim matrix, proof manifest, research backlog,
appendices, and release records.

## Problem

A living book can decay into a pile of references unless it has a research
backlog with admission rules.

New sources will arrive. Some will strengthen an existing layer. Some will
show that a chapter is too broad. Some will be interesting but unread. Some
will contain external citations that need to be checked directly before they
can become this book's citations. Some will report results that cannot be used
until artifacts, commands, environments, and public-release permissions are
available.

The dangerous move is to let all of those states blur together. A title in a
queue is not a source note. A source note is not a reproduced result. A
private artifact is not public evidence. A generated appendix is not a support
transition. A future proof target is not a theorem about the deployed system.

The agenda exists to prevent that blur.

## Why A Static Bibliography Is Not Enough

A normal bibliography tells readers where ideas came from. It does not usually
tell a future writing agent what it is allowed to do next.

For this book, that next-step information is part of the architecture. The
manifest tells the system where the chapter belongs. The outline tells a
future agent what to load first. Appendix G separates Corben-authored,
Corben-supplied, recovered, and local-project sources from outside literature.
Appendix H separates external sources by other authors and organizations.
Appendix C records the current claim/support boundary. Source notes record
what has actually been reviewed. The research backlog records what remains
blocked.

Without that state, the book would drift. A chapter might cite a source
because the source appears in a table. A paper might create a duplicate
chapter even though it only renames an existing interface. A benchmark claim
might sound stronger because it sits near a benchmark source. An external
reference named inside a Corben paper might become a citation before the
reference itself is checked.

That is how a living book becomes a confident scrapbook.

The agenda also protects the reader. The final chapter should not bury the
book's uncertainty behind a polished ending. It should say what is still open
in a way that makes the next action executable: fetch the source, write the
note, normalize the citation, add the fixture, formalize the predicate, run
the benchmark, import the artifact, update the chapter, or close the item as
out of scope.

## Core Claim

The bibliography and research agenda should map source families, external
literature queues, missing papers, proof targets, experiment backlogs, and
chapter insertion rules.

In the live AI/research book, that remains a design rationale at `argument`
support. The repository has source inventory records, source notes, chapter
source queues, exact claim-source mappings, passage-reviewed mapping refs for
the assigned sources, protocol fixtures, and finite Lean hooks. Those are
real governance surfaces. They do not prove citation accuracy, external
literature completeness, source-interpretation quality, new-paper triage
quality, benchmark reproduction, artifact reproduction, or chapter truth.

That boundary is the point. The agenda is evidence discipline for future
work, not proof that future work has already been done.

## The Research Graph

The mature object is a research graph, not a list.

Each node in the graph has an identity and a state. A source can be
discovered, inventoried, cached, connector-readable, source-noted,
passage-reviewed, mapped to claims, integrated into prose, tested,
mechanized, reproduced, deprecated, refuted, blocked, or rejected. A chapter
can be canonical live prose, generated reader output, curated reader
derivative, companion-note material, or release-approved artifact. A claim can
remain at `argument`, move through an accepted evidence transition, or stay
blocked by missing evidence.

Those states are not bureaucracy. They are how the book remembers what it is
allowed to know.

The current final chapter has a deliberately broad source queue because it
governs the backlog for the whole stack. Verification Bandwidth and
Benchmaxxing supply the primary agenda pressure: context adequacy,
semantic-unit verification, contradiction-rate tests, benchmark lifecycles,
residuals, and anti-Goodhart safeguards. Supporting sources such as Stable
Capability Fields, PlanForge, VCM, Spinoza, Talos, RMI, CGS, GenesisCode,
Simulation Scaling, Project Theseus, Circle, and the constitutional sources
name future proof, test, artifact, and governance lanes. Connector and recovery
sources such as MoECOT, CoilMoECOT, Road To AGI, and VCM Editable remain
bounded by their source-note and access status.

The chapter may use those materials to organize the backlog. It may not use
them to imply completed runtime, benchmark, model-quality, source-citation, or
deployment results.

## Intake And Triage

Every new AI paper or artifact should enter through a triage decision before
it changes prose.

The first question is not "is this interesting?" Most things are interesting.
The first question is "what boundary does this affect?" A new source may:

- update an existing chapter;
- add a missing source note or external-literature record;
- create a proof or experiment backlog item;
- propose a new chapter only if it owns a distinct interface, invariant,
  artifact type, or failure mode;
- route to an appendix or companion note;
- remain blocked because source access, public-safety status, or citation
  metadata is not good enough;
- be rejected as duplicate, superseded, out of scope, or unsupported.

That triage rule is what lets the book stay dynamic without becoming
shapeless. A living book should be easy to update, but it should not reward
every new paper with a new chapter. The best use of many sources is to deepen
an existing boundary.

The current repository already has synthetic protocol coverage for this shape:
the new-paper triage scenario fixture checks update-existing,
propose-new-chapter, defer-external-literature, and reject-duplicate cases.
The paired research-backlog fixture names one mapped backlog item with access,
storage, public-safety, chapter-action, deduplication, promotion-blocker, and
non-claim fields. `AsiStackProofs.BibliographyPlan` adds two finite gates and
two negative-case theorems: source-derived support requires a source note or
equivalent ingested-source artifact, and an accepted new-source assignment
requires an existing chapter id. Those fixtures and proofs are useful, but
narrow. They validate record shape, decision categories, and two record-level
failure gates. They do not prove that a future real paper has been judged well.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  Source["New paper, artifact, critique, or missing source"] --> Inventory["Inventory record"]
  Inventory --> Access["Access and public-safety policy"]
  Access --> Note{"Readable and permitted?"}
  Note -- "yes" --> SourceNote["Source note or external source note"]
  Note -- "not yet" --> Backlog["Backlog item with blocker and non-claims"]
  SourceNote --> Boundary{"Existing chapter boundary?"}
  Boundary -- "yes" --> Existing["Update source queue, claim map, or proof/test backlog"]
  Boundary -- "no" --> NewChapter["Propose precise chapter boundary"]
  Boundary -- "unclear" --> Review["Merge/split/defer review"]
  Existing --> Evidence["Evidence transition or no-promotion blocker"]
  NewChapter --> Evidence
  Review --> Evidence
  Backlog --> Evidence
  Evidence --> Release["Changelog, validation, release record if applicable"]

  SourceNote -. "source note is not reproduction" .-> Evidence
  Backlog -. "blocked item is not evidence" .-> Evidence
  Release -. "publication is not support promotion" .-> Evidence
```

The dashed edges are the important controls: source notes are not
reproduction, blocked items are not evidence, and publication is not support
promotion.

## Interfaces

The research agenda is held together by several public interfaces.

`sources/source_inventory.json` gives every accepted source a stable identity.
Source notes record what has actually been read or inspected. Appendix G
contains Corben-side and local-project material. Appendix H contains external
sources by other authors and organizations. `docs/book_outline.md` tells
future writing runs which sources to load for each chapter. `book_structure.json`
keeps parts and chapters stable without manual numbering. Appendix C keeps
claim support visible. The proof manifest and Lean modules record formal
targets. Protocol fixtures record synthetic intake and backlog shapes.

The Research Backlog Record is the agenda's main work order. It should name
the source or gap, access state, storage policy, public-safety state, assigned
chapters, chapter action policy, decision refs, deduplication state, merge or
split policy, source-note state, claim-mapping state, passage-review state,
external-literature need, proof or test backlog, evidence-transition
preconditions, required pre-drafting work, source refs, support-state effect,
promotion blockers, residuals, next action, and non-claims.

That sounds dense because the failure modes are dense. The record exists so a
future assistant can tell the difference between "read this," "cite this,"
"test this," "formalize this," "do not publish this," and "do not promote
this."

## Invariants

The agenda has five hard invariants.

First, do not cite unread sources as support.

Second, do not infer bibliographic metadata. If a source record is incomplete,
the incompleteness is a blocker, not an invitation to guess.

Third, new papers update inventory and triage state before they update chapter
claims.

Fourth, missing or private sources are backlog items, not evidence.

Fifth, new chapters are added only when they own a distinct boundary. The book
is meant to be precise, not inflated.

These invariants also apply to local projects. Project Theseus and Circle are
valuable implementation and proof-contract references, but local availability
does not automatically become public evidence. The book can record static
imports, consumer gates, pinned digests, and bounded receipt slices. It cannot
generalize from those artifacts into broad claims about deployed capability,
model quality, transfer performance, runtime safety, or ASI progress.

## Failure Modes

The most common failure is citation drift. A source starts as a queue item,
then appears in a matrix, then becomes familiar enough that prose treats it as
support even though the relevant passages were never checked. The cure is
state separation: queued, unread, source-noted, passage-reviewed, mapped,
tested, reproduced, and promoted are different states.

The second failure is duplicate expansion. A new paper can bring useful
language without owning a new chapter. If the book creates a chapter for every
new vocabulary family, it becomes an anthology again. The agenda should push
new material toward existing boundaries unless the source really introduces a
new artifact, interface, invariant, or failure mode.

The third failure is backlog rot. Future-work lists can become a place where
hard tasks go to disappear. A serious backlog item needs a next action small
enough to execute: fetch, cache, read, note, cite, test, formalize, reproduce,
revise, reject, or close.

The fourth failure is evidence laundering through publication. A rendered
site, a generated bibliography, a valid schema, a Lean build, a passing
fixture, or a reader artifact can be important. None of those artifacts
automatically changes a chapter core claim. Support moves only through the
claim/evidence discipline recorded elsewhere in the book.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is already visible as a conservative source
governance system: a split Corben/local source appendix and external-source
appendix, source inventory, source notes, source queues, exact claim-source
mappings, proof targets, research backlog schema, new-paper triage scenario,
and validation commands.

The bounded implemented slice is small enough to name exactly: one
`research_backlog_record.valid.json` fixture, one
`new_paper_triage_scenario.valid.json` fixture with four synthetic intake
cases, protocol-schema validation for those records, and two finite Lean gates
with negative cases. The fixture support effect is backlog-only or
record-shape-only. It is not a live paper intake, citation check, literature
normalization pass, chapter-insertion approval, source-interpretation review,
or support-state promotion.

That is enough to stop the worst drift. A new source has somewhere to land. A
future chapter proposal has to name its boundary. External literature stays
separate from Corben-side material. Connector-readable or private material can
inform planning without pretending to be public evidence. The book can record
blocked work without smoothing it into prose.

It is not enough to claim the agenda is complete. The current minimum does not
perform direct citation checks for every external reference, normalize every
outside bibliography trail mined from Corben papers, rehearse a live user
paper intake, reproduce benchmark artifacts, import all private project
results, or prove semantic adequacy of future triage decisions.

The next honest exercise is a live intake run against a real user-provided AI
paper or artifact with public-safety policy, source note, chapter decision,
claim mapping, proof/test backlog, and no-promotion blockers recorded before
any prose change.

## Beyond The State Of The Art

The mature endpoint is an active research graph for the whole ASI Stack.

In that version, every paper, project record, external reference,
conversation-mined packet, artifact, benchmark result, proof target, missing
source, and reader-edition blocker has a durable state. The graph knows what
has been read, what has been checked, what is private, what is source-reported,
what is externally grounded, what has a proof path, what has a replay path,
what has failed, what has been refuted, and what is not allowed to support a
claim.

A future writing agent could bring a new paper to the repository and receive a
bounded decision: update these chapters, add these source notes, do not create
a new chapter, create this proof target, run this fixture, add these external
comparators, leave support unchanged, and record these blockers. A future
reader edition could derive from the same graph without changing claim truth.
A future release could say exactly which source, proof, test, reader, and
audio artifacts were produced and reviewed.

That endpoint would make bibliography part of governance. It would not make
the ASI Stack true by itself. It would make future truth-seeking harder to
fake.

## Test And Proof Boundaries

This chapter has useful executable support, but the support is narrow.

The source inventory and source-note validators check that source records and
notes stay structurally consistent. Protocol validation checks the research
backlog and new-paper triage fixtures. `AsiStackProofs.BibliographyPlan`
proves finite-record gates: a source-derived claim requires a source note or
equivalent ingested-source artifact, and an accepted new-source assignment
requires an existing chapter id.

Those checks are valuable because they prevent specific classes of drift. They
do not prove citation accuracy, literature completeness, reviewer judgment,
chapter-boundary quality, source interpretation, benchmark truth, public
release permission, or future triage wisdom.

The right conclusion is not "the agenda is validated." The right conclusion is
"the first gates exist, and the harder evidence work remains visible."

## Closing The Book Openly

This final chapter hands the reader back to the workflow.

The ASI Stack has been presented as a governed architecture: authority stays
bounded, claims carry evidence states, proofs stay scoped, tests require
artifacts, reader editions derive from the live source, and self-improvement
has to pass through gates. The book should obey the same rule. It should keep
improving only by leaving public-safe traces of what changed, what stayed
speculative, what failed, what was deferred, and what evidence would be needed
to say more.

The ending, then, is not a sealed conclusion. It is a map of responsible next
work. The reader should leave with the central distinction intact: a governed
architecture can be worth building before every claim is proved, but the book
must keep saying exactly which parts are argued, which parts are mechanically
checked, which parts are locally tested, which parts are externally grounded,
and which parts remain open.

## Handoff

Future work should begin at the research graph, not in free-form prose. Add or
update the source record. Write the source note. Decide the chapter boundary.
Record the proof or test route. Keep support unchanged unless an accepted
evidence transition says otherwise. Update the reader surface only after the
live evidence boundary is clear.

That is how the living book remains alive without becoming ungoverned.
